speaker: interviewer
text: --with tape two with Ben Gerstein. Before I ask you about the hiding, I'd like to go back a tiny bit and ask you about your life in 1941. So this was a ghetto that you were living in--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a ghetto [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] without borders, without fences. It was a designated ghetto. And you knew where you were supposed to be living, and past that area you're not-- you're not allowed to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the street boundaries were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Even though we lived-- they also let us live in our own home. They didn't throw us out. And it was separated by-- by a water from the ghetto and the city. Yet we lived in that little area, as I said before, a [? village ?] there in comparison to the city. So there was also a little ghetto there they let us live, and-- and the ghetto in the main city. Almost like two ghettos within the same city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you ever allowed to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. No. No, that was forbidden. You-- the minute you were out past that area you were like-- now it's hunting season, so forget about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did ever-- did people ever try to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one that-- the ones that tried to say, well-- that we-- we left. That's how we survived. But we had to take the chance, because we knew liquidation was coming. And sure enough, it came in four days. You see? But just to leave for the sake of leaving? No way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was food a problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never a problem, really. Yeah, some people were hungrier than others. There was-- the ones who were uprooted from their area and thrown into the ghetto, naturally. They had to live on handouts. But people helped each other. We never-- our own family, and most of our area never had problems because the Ukrainians, the farmers, the Polish farmers, the Czech farmers, still kept coming in, like they did before the invasion, to deal and barter. Most of the stuff was-- was on barter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So business continued as usual?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not quite, but almost. In other words, we weren't starved. We weren't starved. Because we had access to food through-- through the bartering, through the farmers coming in. And, you know, and a pair of shoes for a half of pound of butter, or you had a fur coat for a bag of flour. You know, that type of stuff. And they let-- some of that kept going without any interference.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as a young boy, did you have a-- a particular role to play?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not really. Not really. You mean up until '41? Up until the Germans came In? Or after '41? You're talking about the ghetto part?

speaker: interviewer
text: The ghetto part, in '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing other than the voluntary. I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had, as I said before, a very tall home. It was like a-- one, two, three-- three and a half stories-- three stories. And if we ran up to the attic we could see almost the whole-- all, the whole neighborhood. It was the tallest home. And across the road we had a-- a Polish baron. He ran away before the Russians invaded. And he had a huge estate of about 100 acres, 150 acres, and almost a palace type of a home. And when the Germans came in, they set up a military post, that type of thing. And I remember sitting up on top of that window looking over what they were doing. And there were wagons-- not trucks, wagons, like hay-- hay wagons with horses. And the Polish farmers and Russian soldiers used to ride them. Sometimes there was a blanket over them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a lot of times nothing. They were Russian corpses-- prisoner of war corpses. Day in and day out. And I remembered like I'm looking at you now, they were pulling it by the leg at the end, and into the graves, into mass graves. Just like dead chickens [? you're ?] [? done, ?] [? through ?] with, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and there was-- for a little while there, there was a Russian crew. So again, soldiers of POWs. And-- and they were working with a German guard, or two German guards-- maybe four or five of them. And I came over and I gave one a piece of chocolate that I had with me. And the German let me have it. Then he calls me over. He says, better yet, why don't you ask your mother if she can give him some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked at him, and said, can I do that? He says don't make it too obvious, but yeah. So I came home, and I told my mother. So she got to run to another few neighbors. And we-- she made him a soup, a bowl of soup like-- like five gallons, and bread and stuff like that. And we headed slowly. And we put [? him ?] on a spot, and we told the Russians where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think the German soldier sat down to have dinner with them. That's how he turned out. He happened to be a good man. And we fed them like that for about three, four weeks until they finished their job, and they had to move somewhere else. And the irony is that one of those four crews, when we got liberated, he came looking for me. And he found us. Yeah. One of the four survived. So that's how things happen sometimes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a great vantage point from your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had a vantage point. I could see for weeks and weeks corpses from the prison where they kept the Russian prisons. And they were just dying off like flies-- disease, I don't think they fed them. Same thing as a concentration camps, basically. No hospitals-- nothing. Hardly any food. And they just kept dying off. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you see from up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically that's it, because that-- you know, that's all there was to-- to see [? their ?] [? eyes. ?] We saw incidents. One German got drunk and he was beating up a priest. I'll never forget that-- that incident. And the priest kept showing him the cross. He took the cross, throw it away. I saw him throw it away. He kept beating him up until another German happened to be bicycling by. He stopped. And he stopped him from beating up the priest. Don't ask me how or why or what. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So little things. I remember Germans stopping two Jews and cutting their beards off with part of the skin-- you know, the religious Jews. These were the [? incidences. ?] This was more later on, you know, after the occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? then we ?] started to get bad when the SS, the special units, started to come-- the liquidation units. So we knew it was coming towards the end. We were about 26,000 Jews living in Lutsk-- 27,000. The liquidation-- I have somewhere a book about Lutsk, about the history of our town. It's written in Jewish. And that's-- the facts are in there. And they killed 24,000 Jews in three days. Mass graves just outside our-- our city, maybe five kilometers, three kilometers, four kilometers. You know, we are hiding out. We heard the machine guns going for three solid days. Lining up, getting undressed, the usual thing. Getting undressed, standing around the grave, machine gun them. And that's how they liquidated 24,000 Jews. One of my friends-- my sister's age, actually; two years older, three years older-- he was part of the liquidation people. Loaded them on the truck, guards, to the graves, machine gun them. And it was getting dusk. When they machine gun, I don't know, 20, 30 people lining up by the-- against the grave, a man was standing next to them. And as they start machine gunning from one end, he pushed him-- he was a young boy, as I said; that time he was 14 or so-- he fell down. He fell. And there must've been four rows, or ten rows liquidated by that time. And they fell with the dead people.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd say 70% of them, 60%, were injured not dead. You know what I'm saying? So that's-- but they got buried anyhow. And they put in earth, and some like a limestone, a light spray of limestone, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: It started to get night. And the Germans, they could hear. They checked over the graves. Nobody moving. And a lot of times they keep shooting anyhow, you know, just to make sure. When it got dark they could see. And they start to moving about an hour later. They're almost choking to death. But they didn't hear any shooting, so they figured it must be safe to-- And they were under one-- oh yeah, the were under one row of bodies. So they moved out the bodies, this man and that kid. And they started crawling out. And the man made the kid stand up on his shoulder, because he was-- it was about 12 feet deep, 14 feet deep. Something like that. And he crawled out-- the kid crawled out. And then he went and got a branch, something like that, I forget how he told me the story. And he helped the man out of the grave. And he was wandering from little farm, knocking on door to have a piece of bread, something. And that boy, which used to be a friend of mine, he survived the war from the grave. He joined us for a little while. Then we had to run each our own way. We-- the liquidation happened in 19-- end of 1942, late in '42-- fall. And so we-- my father sent us to that farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was before you went into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that-- yeah, that's just before we went into hiding. But out of the city, out of the liquidated ghetto, you see? We ran away four days before the liquidation happened. Because of the tip-off of my aunt. And we ran away to that little town where there was a little farmers' village where the German had a halfway point I was explaining to you before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a little-- in the village there used to be a big, huge farmhouse. And they [? sat ?] [? out ?] there. They're like a what they call a halfway house for Germans. It was on the main road from one city to the other. So when a battalion drove by, 200 people, 300, they'd just put them up and feed them. And then they can go into the front or whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they needed workers. And he didn't care they were Jews or not Jews. And most of them were Jews of course. And he didn't get the order to liquidate them, see? So my father found out about it, and we were working there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Polish farmer-- or no, Ukrainian farmer-- he agreed with my father, we're going to keep them there just for a little while until over-- the liquidation's over, and then we would have to find a place somewhere else. Because if you were found to hide a Jew the whole family would be killed and the farm burned. That was the retribution.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have to escape, then, from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We [? had-- ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we went in a horse and buggy at night. Yeah. With a horse and buggy at night. That's right. And we arrived in that little village overnight. We-- we drove all night, and he arrived at that little village. I forget now how far. It couldn't have been because dusk was, I don't know, 10 o'clock, or so. 10 or 11. That's when he put our whole family-- my father-- dressed like real farmers. He-- We had a horse. And he bought a buggy, or rented a buggy. I forget now how that happened. And he took us to that little town-- to that little village-- farmers' village. And we stayed there for about a month and a half. And from there we knew it was just a matter of a day before we had to liquidate them. He got the order, and that had to be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: What possessions did you take when you were leaving by horse and buggy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. A coat, a hat, clothes-- that's about it. You had to be mobile.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in fall. August-- September, October-- October by now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fall of 194--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of 1942. Of 1942? No. '43. Pardon me. '43. Yeah. And-- and then we left that farmer. And-- and sure enough, two days later, they liquidated that-- that little camp that a few Jews were working. And we found a Polish farmer. And we paid him to do that. It was not a favor. It was like a business transaction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Our problem was we had trouble sleeping at night, because a few things happened to-- to Jews that gave that little money that they had-- whatever they have-- to the Polish farmer or Ukrainian farmer. And they found them the next morning hacked to death, including the money that they kept. You see what I'm saying? So-- because there was no crime. Anybody can do that. So that happened quite a bit. So almost-- one of us had to be awake at night. Not-- I don't know what we could have done against it, because we only had really-- we were hiding with my immediate family. It was only my father by that time. So he couldn't do much against a farmer and his sons, and, you know, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow we didn't get into any problems there. Very little food. We were hungry most of the time-- especially [? when we're ?] sitting and doing nothing all day. We-- he made us a spot in a haystack-- straw stack-- pardon me. Huge, huge straw stack-- about, oh, I don't know. 25, 30 feet wide. And it went into a point like a pyramid. And he made a little secret trap door. And this is where the five of us were-- myself, my sister, and my father, my mother, and my mother's sister. That's the one that tipped us off about the-- Oh yeah, pardon me. Then my father's sister, her husband, and her son-- young boy-- found us also in that village, in the farmers' village. And they stuck with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the haystack?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the haystack. So there were nine of us. Four-- pardon me. Four of us, five of us, eight of us. Nine of us with my cousin, the one that's in New York. I'm forgetting the numbers now. Nine of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mula. That was her original husband in Polish, Udlis-- U-D-L-I-S-- and Basia. So Basia, Mula, and Mark Udlis. These are Polish names. And so there was the three of them, my aunt Jenny Shostack, maiden name; my cousin, same age as I, Bruce Gerstein. We both have the same name-- named after the-- the grandfather. So there was the nine of us hiding in that haystack for six months. There was a wash room. That was a-- everything. We did everything there.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a washroom there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I'm saying that was our washroom. The room was our washroom, was our toilet, our feeding ground, our-- our living quarters, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could not go out of that haystack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could not go out. You couldn't even talk. We had to talk in complete whispers, because haystack carries-- sound carries out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it in a barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the temperature?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cold. [CHUCKLING] Cold. We went through one winter there. But not as cold as being completely outside, because hay keeps some insulation a little bit. It started to get worse and worse. He started not to feed us at all. And then he told us to get going. He doesn't want us anymore. The Germans will find [? us, ?] they'll kill him, and the farm out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just backtracking, but how did he bring food to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a trapdoor from a certain side. It was covered with hay. you know. And he would open up the trap door and hand us the food. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times a day did he do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once. Once a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And give me an example of a typical meal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soups. A little bit of bread. Maybe once in a blue moon a piece of pork, or something like that, if the pig was killed at that particular time. Very little. It was rough. That-- that part was rough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the farmers name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember his name. I don't remember his name. But you know, if-- if-- I don't know. Is there God above? If there is such a thing, or-- or faith has it, his trying to get us out of that-- of his place-- saved our live. Because it was a free fall. The Ukrainians were against Polacks, because Ukrainia was never a country as Ukraine. It was always Poland. And Ukraine was Ukraine, but never an independent country. They were occupied by Poland or by Russia or by the Austrians or by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were naturally-- natural enemies with the Polacks. Against Jews, everybody had the free for all. But-- when-- when the Germans came in, the Germans told the Ukrainians to cooperate with the Germans, and they'll give them a free Ukraine. So at first they believed it, and they cooperated with them. And at the same time, they helped themselves to get revenge on the Polacks, you see, for all the bad things they've done [? them ?] before. So they were killing them almost as-- as easy as they were the killing-- not quite-- but just about as they were killing the Jews they were killing the Polacks. Four days after we left, the Polacks-- we had to, we had no choice-- they found us a place. They found us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my aunt Udlis, the two of them went out dressed completely as typically Ukrainian farmers. And they went looking for a hiding place for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does a typical Ukrainian farmer look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a big kerchief, and a black-- always black dress. And you know-- what does a farmer look different from a [? farm ?] than it does [INAUDIBLE]. You know what I'm saying. Equating it is the same idea. And-- and they found us a place with a Czech family-- Czechoslovakian. They agreed to take us. Again, we had to give them something. But it was minor-- a minor thing. I think-- I think the farmer, him, he did it because he just wanted to help us. We stayed there nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you gave him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we gave him I think it was a couple of gold coins to make teeth. That's basically why people wanted gold-- to make teeth. Gold teeth, you see. And-- because you could always handle-- gold was the thing to survive with. Nothing else. Diamond didn't mean anything. Gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to take some of that from your home?

words.text: some of that from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a few pieces with us. Whatever we had. Mostly it got buried back in our house. We couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you bury it physically in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We buried it, and then we couldn't find it. We couldn't find it. So just what-- what my father had lying around, quickly he grabbed [INAUDIBLE]. And we stayed there for nine months. After six months of staying there, they couldn't take the pressure, because they found a few-- they found out a few farmers that kept Jews. They killed them and burned their whole family-- the whole family. The whole family. They killed the whole family and burned the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that she became hysterical, completely. She couldn't sleep and they came to us to beg us to leave. We'll give you back the gold pieces. We'll give you back. We'll give you food to last you as much as you can carry. Leave us. We have our own children. Why should I take a chance? So my father somehow talked to them and talked to them. And he explained to them we will not say a word if we get caught, if we leave. But what of the kids? Either he gives them a slap on the ass or gives them a chocolate. They're young kids. In other words, he-- he put in a different [? word ?] with them. Somehow he talked them into not-- putting still up with us for-- for a little while. And then he heard that the Russians were starting to beat back the Germans. And so he had a little more encouragement, because how long can you keep it? And sure enough, we got liberated. He didn't let-- want to let us out until he was sure they were the Russians, because he didn't-- because there were rumors that some of the Ukrainians got dressed as Russians, speaking Russians, and say where did-- any Jews in here? Any Jews in here? We have liberated you. And then when they found the Jews-- apparently that happened-- they killed them. So he had to-- so he was so [? careful. ?] He said I kept you up till now. He says, I don't want to take any chances now. And he waited for almost two weeks after we were liberated, until the second wave of Russians came in. And then he said to us, how do we get now into the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, he dressed us-- [? you're talking ?] like farmers. Typical. He gave us his horse, a sleigh. Nine of us. Eh-- at that time it was five-- five of us, because they found their own farmer-- my cousin, my aunt, and her son, and her husband found a different Czechoslovakian farm. We had to split up after the Polish farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this farmer gave us-- our farmer gave us a horse, a sleigh. And we drove into the city where the Russians already had it occupied. And that's when we got liberated. That was in early 1945. Do you remember the exact date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the exact date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know it was winter. We were in a sleigh. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think it was winter '45. February, January, February-- something like that. Early '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Just going back a bit. So when you were hiding in the second place, where were you staying actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that's another story. I hope the tape doesn't run out. We were hiding on a-- in an attic of the farm, there were-- of the barn. There were cows on the bottom. There was an attic above, and we had a ladder. We would climb up and hide. And I sort of-- he made us a spot. Then he found out-- when this farmer, that they kept Jews-- as I told you-- two farms that he found out that they were completely burned to the ground and killed, he started to really get frightened. And since he decided not to [INAUDIBLE], not to let us go, didn't throw us out-- after that little meeting, about a month later. And every time the-- the signal came around. There was some Germans driving by. Ukrainians are looking around. Right away we ran. He took the ladder away from the farm. He hid us in the-- [INAUDIBLE] well, we knew how to get into-- into that corner of the stack of the hay in the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyhow, four weeks after he decided to let us stay, we knew he was doing something. Somehow we-- we heard him working in the barn, downstairs where the cows are. We didn't know what he was doing, because he always worked at night. And we trusted him explicitly. So we weren't worried about that. But why? What's he doing? One day he says to us, I have a present for you. So my father said, what is it? He says, surprise. Come on. And we all crawled down from the farm-- from the barn. And underneath the manger of where the cows eat, you know, [? the ?] manger, he pulled up a little string there and opened up a trap door. Trap door was enough to slide down for a body.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told my father to slide down. My father slide down. He gave him a candle. And then he called us to slide down with us. And we come, and we crawled into a little space. I don't know, it was-- it was five feet by five feet wide. It had one step, I remember. Step and out. It was very, very tiny. It's-- it's [? one ?] you couldn't sit five people. We could hardly. We had to set [INAUDIBLE] us. We had to [? stand ?] [? up on ?] my father's lap-- something like. He told us that's going to be our hiding place in case of emergency. And he dug that by hand himself. And putting on a haystack-- and putting on his horse and buggy, and taking it out to the field, and spreading the earth with the other earth. He built that. And that was our lifesaver. Because I remember once, the Germans came in. And they were sort of looking. Somebody told them. But they didn't come specially looking. They came in because they were passing by, [? whether ?] somebody told the Ukrainian, he saw a couple of Jews running around looking for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they did a search right through all the farms-- especially in our from farm. They stayed there. We were sitting there. So we ran down quickly into it. What was the signal?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the-- one of the older girls came in. I showed you in the picture there. The older girl came running in. 'Down.' She didn't say nothing else, just, 'Down.' And we went down. And then we put the trap door over us. The Germans came in, and started looking for us. And they told the Czech, if you give us-- if there is anybody hiding, you tell us, and we'll spare you. Which is a lie. They never do anyhow. They would never spare him, but.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to stop tape two here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to stop tape two here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
